ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Nasi Tomato; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice dish' (FOODON_03311069) as the most defensible ancestor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Nasi Tomato. Mapped to the rice-based mixed dish category (A000E) as the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Nasi Tomato or a sufficiently close rice dish analogue.

preparation_method: cooked with tomato purée and evaporated milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nasi Tomato is approximately 250 g, equivalent to roughly one heaped plate of rice as typically served at a Malay festive meal or wedding banquet.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Mixed Malaysian Dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia — MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD for mixed Malaysian rice dishes. Nasi Tomato is a recognised Malaysian dish; values represent a composite estimate for the cooked rice dish including tomato purée, evaporated milk, and whole spices. Saturated fat estimated from ghee/evaporated milk content typical of this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data are not individually reported in the IMR MyFCD source for this dish and are therefore set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white basmati rice, cooked, analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Tomato. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy to cooked white basmati rice (GI ~58–64) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The inclusion of fat from ghee/evaporated milk and the acidity of tomato purée are expected to modestly attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 71.25) / 100 = 42.75, rounded to 42.8. Confidence: 0.70.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 180 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 450 mg sodium, representing roughly 20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced salt during preparation, and pair with potassium-rich sides such as vegetables to help offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (60) and high glycemic load per serving (~42.8) driven by refined long-grain or basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate source; a 250 g serving provides approximately 71 g of carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to 100–150 g, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response individually.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and evaporated milk (estimated 1.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium (180 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors in this dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using vegetable oil instead of ghee, use low-fat evaporated milk, control portion size to 150–200 g, and ensure the overall meal is low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lycopene from tomato purée and anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon) may provide modest benefit; however, tomatoes are nightshade vegetables which may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume Nasi Tomato in moderate portions; those who identify nightshade sensitivity should trial elimination and monitor joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Tomato (Tomato Rice) is a fragrant, vibrantly coloured Malay rice dish prepared by cooking long-grain rice — most commonly basmati — in a flavourful base of tomato purée, evaporated milk or coconut milk, and whole aromatic spices including cinnamon sticks, star anise, cardamom pods, and cloves. Sautéed shallots, garlic, and ginger are incorporated to build a deep, layered flavour. The finished rice has a slightly tangy, creamy, and savoury profile with a warm orange-red hue. It is a staple of Malay festive cuisine, traditionally served at weddings, Hari Raya celebrations, and special family gatherings, almost always accompanied by Ayam Masak Merah (Spicy Tomato Chicken) and Acar Jelatah (Pickled Cucumber Salad). Nutritionally, a 250 g serving provides approximately 413 kcal, 8 g protein, 11 g fat, and 71 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~60) and a high glycemic load per serving (~42.8), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at 180 mg per 100 g. The dish is halal and gluten-free but contains dairy from evaporated milk. Tomato purée contributes lycopene and vitamin C, while whole spices provide trace antioxidants. It is not suitable for strict low-carbohydrate or weight-loss diets without significant portion reduction.
common_user_queries: What is served with nasi tomato?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD for mixed Malaysian dishes, which is an approved primary regional source. The match is adapted rather than exact, as Nasi Tomato is a composite dish with variable preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked white basmati rice as the reference food. Saturated fat is estimated from typical ghee and evaporated milk content. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data are not individually reported in the source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for Nasi Tomato are not individually reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore databases as of 2024; these fields were confirmed unavailable upon review of both sources. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per IMR source for this rice-based dish with no significant vitamin D-contributing ingredients. Saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from typical ghee and evaporated milk proportions in standard Nasi Tomato recipes; this value should be verified against direct laboratory analysis when available. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white basmati rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Nasi Tomato was found in the approved source set. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (60 × 71.25) / 100 = 42.75, rounded to 42.8, based on 250 g default serving providing 71.25 g carbohydrate. The dish is almost always served with Ayam Masak Merah and Acar Jelatah; the nutritional values here represent the rice component only. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, as these are the closest ancestor classes available and no exact match exists for Nasi Tomato in either ontology.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Nasi Tomato', the canonical English/Malay name for this dish. Local names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi satisfying the Malay cuisine multilingual requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (165.0), protein_g (3.2), fat_g (4.5), and carbohydrate_g (28.5) are all present, numeric, and sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD, an approved primary regional source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.8), sodium_mg (180.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8), and sugar_g (1.5) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (60), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (42.8), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked white basmati rice as the reference, with estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to FOODON_03311069 (rice dish, closest_parent match) with gap note. FoodEx2 mapped to A000E (rice-based mixed dish, closest_parent match) with gap note. Both match types are now set to 'closest_parent', which is within the allowed set ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none']. SNOMED CT set to null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (high), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 10 entries covering English variants, Malay variants, Chinese script (simplified and traditional), Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated and consistent with the flat aliases array. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array: 'Nasi Tomato' ✓, 'Tomato Rice' ✓, 'Malay Tomato Rice' ✓, 'Tomato Basmati Rice' ✓, '番茄饭' ✓, '番茄飯' ✓, 'Nasi tomato' ✓, 'Nasi Tomato Kahwin' ✓, 'தக்காளி சாதம்' ✓, 'टमाटर चावल' ✓.

Iteration 2: Corrected g5_ontology_honesty failures by changing foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' (not in allowed set) to 'closest_parent' (allowed value per validator). Both are now within the permitted set ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Verified all -1 sentinel values are documented in notes with reason and source citation. Confirmed ontology match types are 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2. Confirmed all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array. No further structural issues detected.
